name: Cystatin C (Cys-C)
summary: Cystatin C is a small protein produced by virtually all nucleated cells at a steady rate, freely filtered by the kidneys, and used as a marker of kidney filtration. Because its production is largely independent of muscle mass, diet, age, and sex, it can be a more reliable filtration marker than creatinine in certain people.
whatItMeans: Rising cystatin C suggests the kidneys are filtering less, and it often detects early decline 1–2 years sooner than creatinine because it begins climbing once filtration has fallen by roughly a quarter. It is especially useful when creatinine is unreliable — for example in people with unusually high or low muscle mass, the elderly, or those on creatine supplements. A cystatin-C-based eGFR can be calculated and is sometimes combined with creatinine for a more accurate estimate. This is educational information, not medical advice.
raises: Reduced kidney filtration (acute or chronic kidney disease)
Obesity, smoking, and chronic inflammation (modest effects)
confounders: Unlike creatinine, cystatin C is independent of muscle mass and creatine supplementation, so it does not falsely rise in muscular or supplementing individuals
Thyroid dysfunction and high-dose glucocorticoids can shift cystatin C independent of true kidney function
Inflammation and obesity may modestly raise it without filtration loss
Pairing cystatin C with creatinine (combined eGFR) generally outperforms either marker alone

ranges.note: General adult reference range; reported intervals vary by assay (e.g., ~0.57–1.05 or ~0.62–1.15 mg/L). Values trend slightly higher with age. Source: clinical laboratory references (LabCorp; PMC reviews of cystatin C as a CKD marker).

references.title: PMC — Cystatin C as a biomarker of chronic kidney disease
NIDDK — Estimating GFR (eGFR) and Kidney Function
